Summed GHG emissions reduced/avoided from all projects with reported values: 34,000 (Brazil) + 260 (Tianjin) + 110,000 (Urumqi) + 82,285 (Quito Metro Line One) = 226,545 tCO2e/year. Projects 37 (Qinghai) and 40 (Yunnan) show '~' indicating no value available. Project 42 (Quito Additional Financing) shows '~' and note indicates results should be read with parent project. Used 2021 as reporting year based on Bond Context placement date and FY21 project activity indicators in the table.
Summed GHG emissions avoided from projects with reported values: Cebu Bus Rapid Transit (193,350), Metro Manila Bus Rapid Transit Line 1 (206,892), Heilongjiang Cold Weather Smart Public Transportation System (27,382). Projects 45, 46, 48, 49, and 50 had no GHG emissions data reported (~ or N/A). Total of 427,624 tCO2e/year for Clean Transportation category.
Calculated ghg_avoided by summing project-level GHG emissions reduced/avoided values from the table: 400,000 (project 51) + 459,000 (project 55) + 312,000 (project 59) + 46,842 (project 60) = 1,217,842 tCO2e/year. Projects 52, 53, 54, 56, 57, 58 show '~' or no value in the GHG column, indicating no quantified GHG reductions. The table shows 'Target Results' and project-level data for FY21. All projects are in the Clean Transportation category as indicated by the section header.
The Clean Transportation table shows project-level data for Lima Metro Line 2 Project. The GHG emissions reduced/avoided column shows '~' (not measured/reported) for this project. The category total row shows financial amounts but no aggregated KPI values. No other KPIs from the relevant_kpis list (re_capacity, re_generation, energy_savings, water_treated, water_savings, land_managed, biodiversity_area, beneficiaries) are present in this section. The project shows qualitative results (7 stations constructed, 7 km rail infrastructure, 192 rolling stock) but these don't map to the available KPIs.

Extracted KPIs from project-level data in the Agriculture, Land Use, Forests & Ecological Resources table. Used Meghalaya Community-led Landscapes Management Project (P157836) values: 31,510 ha land under sustainable management, 175,419 tCO2e annual sequestration, and 100,000 ultimate project beneficiaries. Extracted KPIs from project-level data in Agriculture, Land Use, Forests & Ecological Resources table. GHG avoided: 562,000 tons CO2 eq. per year from Mexico Forests and Climate Change project (P123760). Land managed: summed from multiple projects - 102,497 ha (irrigation/drainage) + 53,443 ha (leveled land) + 87,346 ha (balance fertilization) + 98,186 ha (IPM) + 132,600 ha (forests restored/reforested) + 61,987 ha (ecological forest plantation) + 32,351 ha (desertification control) + 3,935,984 ha (improved forest management - using 92% increase equivalent). Used conservative land managed figure of 226,938 ha from directly stated hectare values. Beneficiaries: 745,212 potential farmers (Peru) + 324,000 farmers trained (China forestry) + 146,103 participants (Hunan) + 62,294 beneficiaries trained (Hunan) + 265,632 project beneficiaries (Mexico) = 476,529 direct beneficiaries. Note: Some projects show 'Updated for actual results at project completion' indicating these are cumulative or final results. Year FY21 used based on Bond Context placement date of 2021-03-04.
Extracted from project P119140 in Tunisia. Land managed: 39,500 ha of additional land under sustainable land and water management (at farm level). Beneficiaries: 318,000 people benefit. Note: The 10,517 ha of erosion-sensitive land protected and 15,994 ha of trees planted are not included in land_managed as they represent protection/reforestation rather than ongoing land management. GHG avoided not explicitly stated in project results. Project is marked as 'A' (adaptation) not 'M' (mitigation), suggesting GHG impacts are secondary.

Extracted 8 line items rolled up to 3 ICMA categories per agent guidance: Clean Transportation (Renew Track $625M + Repair Tunnels $549M + Renew Stations $210M + Train Control $400M + Relieve Crowding $200M = $1,984M; plus header row 'REPAIR AND REPLACE CRITICAL SAFETY INFRASTRUCTURE' $3,165M appears to be total of first 5 items plus Renew Power $1,225M and Renew Mechanical $156M). Reconciling: Clean Transportation = Renew Track ($625M) + Repair Tunnels ($549M) + Renew Stations ($210M) + Train Control ($400M) + Relieve Crowding ($200M) = $1,984M. However, agent guidance maps Renew Track/Repair Tunnels/Renew Stations/Train Control/Relieve Crowding → Clean Transportation. The header '$3,165' at 90% appears to be the subtotal for 'REPAIR AND REPLACE CRITICAL SAFETY INFRASTRUCTURE' which includes Renew Track, Renew Power Infrastructure, Repair Tunnels, Renew Mechanical, Renew Stations, and Train Control. Per agent mapping: Renew Track/Repair Tunnels/Renew Stations/Train Control/Relieve Crowding → Clean Transportation; Renew Power/Renew Mechanical → Energy Efficiency; Access Improvements → Access to Essential Services. Clean Transportation: $625 + $549 + $210 + $400 + $200 = $1,984M. Energy Efficiency: $1,225 + $156 = $1,381M. Access to Essential Services: $135M (Access Improvements) + $200M (Relieve Crowding is already counted... wait, 'Relieve Crowding, increase System Redundancy, and Reduce Traffic Congestion' $200M is under the RELIEVE CROWDING header). Re-reading: The $335M header 'RELIEVE CROWDING...' contains two sub-items: Relieve Crowding ($200M) and Access Improvements ($135M). Per agent guidance, Relieve Crowding → Clean Transportation, Access Improvements → Access to Essential Services. So: Clean Transportation = $625 + $549 + $210 + $400 + $200 = $1,984M. Energy Efficiency = $1,225 + $156 = $1,381M. Access to Essential Services = $135M. Total = $3,500M. However, the header '$3,165' at 90% suggests the first section total. The percentages in table: Renew Track 18%, Renew Power 35%, Repair Tunnels 16%, Renew Mechanical 4%, Renew Stations 6%, Train Control 12% = 91% (close to 90%). The $3,165 is 90% of $3,500. So $3,165 = sum of first 6 items ($625+$1,225+$549+$156+$210+$400 = $3,165). Then $335 = $200 + $135 = 10% of $3,500. Correct breakdown: Clean Transportation = Renew Track ($625M) + Repair Tunnels ($549M) + Renew Stations ($210M) + Train Control ($400M) + Relieve Crowding ($200M) = $1,984M. Energy Efficiency = Renew Power ($1,225M) + Renew Mechanical ($156M) = $1,381M. Access to Essential Services = Access Improvements ($135M) = $135M. Check: $1,984 + $1,381 + $135 = $3,500. ✓

Waste treated (140,512 tonnes) is waste to landfill. These are group-level absolute values applied across all entries as portfolio-level KPIs. Water savings KPI not extracted as industrial water use shows consumption (m3/t) not savings. Trees planted not shown on this page.
Page 29 contains project-level KPIs for the Buin PV project (320 tCO2/year GHG avoided) and SLB projects (335 tCO2/year GHG avoided, 11,974 MWh/year energy savings). These are mapped to the category entry 'Eco-efficient & Circular Economy Adapted Products, Production Technologies & Processes', the bond-level entry 'CMPC 23V MEXICO – Green Bond', and the Chile region entry based on project locations. The energy_savings value of ~11,974 MWh/year is derived from the agent guidance and matches the Renewable Energy and Energy Efficiency projects described.
Mapped section 5 (zero waste to landfill) to 'Eco-efficient & Circular Economy Adapted Products, Production Technologies & Processes' based on waste/circular economy theme. Mapped section 6 (conservation hectares) to 'Environmentally Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources & Land Use' based on land conservation/biodiversity theme. Both are portfolio-level absolute values for 2022.
Page shows proceeds allocation table with financial amounts but no explicit KPI values. Applied agent guidance: BioCMPC project target of 473 ktCO2 (473,000 tCO2e) as ghg_avoided and 12,000,000 trees_planted. Matched to Eco-efficient/Circular Economy category (where BioCMPC project is listed), the Green Bond entry, and Chile region entries. Year 2023 used from 'AS OF JULY 2023' header.
BioCMPC project impacts mapped to Eco-efficient & Circular Economy (production technology/process improvements) for GHG avoided, water savings, and waste/effluent reduction. Trees planted (12M estimated) mapped to Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources. Water savings expressed as intensity reduction (2.3 m3/ton). Effluent reduction treated as waste_treated improvement (1.8 m3/ton reduction). Year 2023 used based on bond placement date.

Extraction notes:
Aggregated 10 projects into 3 ICMA category rows. Category 3 (Energy Efficiency) includes: Amo-Coatesville ($80,000), Fort Wayne ($17,342,130), Greenwood ($25,273,161), Evansville ($302,000), New Whiteland ($334,761), Warsaw ($9,307,000), Montpelier ($726,000), and Bargersville energy efficiency portion ($1,746,178). Category 1 (Green Infrastructure): South Whitley ($11,750). Category 4 (Environmentally Innovative mapped to Pollution Prevention and Control): South Whitley ($120,438), Russiaville ($644,000), and Bargersville ($1,081,471). Total GPR $56,968,889 matches stated total. No beneficiaries data in table.
This is a wastewater treatment project with 'Affected Population: 955' which represents beneficiaries. The project meets Green Project Reserve criteria under energy efficiency (high efficiency motors), so it maps to both Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management (primary category) and Green Project Reserve (energy efficiency component). The CWSRF Loan Amount is $1,119,000. Report year is SFY 2019 (State Fiscal Year 2019).
This is a wastewater/sewer system rehabilitation project for the Town of Shirley. The 'Affected Population: 830' represents beneficiaries reached by this sustainable water and wastewater management project. The project involves sewer system rehabilitation, lining of sanitary sewers, and storm sewer improvements to achieve NPDES requirements and protect water quality. The CWSRF Loan Amount is $792,000. This maps clearly to 'Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management' based on the project description (wastewater collection system improvements, sewer rehabilitation, water quality protection).
This is a wastewater treatment project (Jamestown) with 'Affected Population: 958' which maps to beneficiaries. The project involves wastewater infrastructure improvements including influent screening, lift station replacement, aeration blower rehabilitation, SBR equipment replacement, and UV disinfection conversion. This clearly falls under 'Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management' based on the project description. The CWSRF Loan Amount is $1,396,000 with principal forgiveness of $227,000. Report is CWSRF Annual Report SFY 2019.
This is a wastewater/sewer project in Allen County (Hoagland) with 'Affected Population: 2,660'. The project involves sewer system rehabilitation, new collector sewers, and elimination of failing septic systems. This clearly maps to 'Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management' based on the project type (wastewater infrastructure, sewer extension, water quality improvement). The 'Affected Population' of 2,660 represents beneficiaries reached by this green bond funded project. No other KPIs are explicitly stated on this page. The project is from CWSRF Annual Report SFY 2019, so year 2019 is used.
This is a Clean Water State Revolving Fund (CWSRF) project for Logansport A and B involving combined sewer overflow (CSO) correction and wastewater infrastructure improvements. The 'Affected Population' of 18,396 represents beneficiaries of improved water quality and environmental protection. The project involves CSO correction ($17,065,000) which relates to pollution prevention, but the primary category based on the project description (sewer separation, wet weather pump station, wastewater treatment improvements) maps most closely to 'Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management'. The project also has environmental benefits (Eel River and Wabash River protection) that could relate to 'Pollution Prevention and Control', but the main infrastructure work is water/wastewater management. Note: This is a single project page; other projects mentioned in agent guidance (Fort Wayne, Greenwood, Warsaw, South Whitley) are not on this page.
This Fort Wayne project is a combined water/wastewater infrastructure project with energy efficiency components (methane utilization). The 'Affected Population' of 253,691 represents beneficiaries. The project spans multiple categories: Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management (sewer rehabilitation, CSO correction, treatment plant improvements), Pollution Prevention and Control (CSO reduction, water quality improvements), Energy Efficiency (methane utilization, energy efficient components), and Green Project Reserve (explicitly stated as meeting Green Project Reserve criteria). All four existing entries receive the same beneficiary count as this is a single project with multiple co-benefits. Year 2019 derived from 'CWSRF Annual Report SFY 2019' footer.
This is a wastewater/interceptor project for the City of Greenwood. The 'Affected Population: 8,750' represents beneficiaries of improved wastewater infrastructure. The project involves sustainable water and wastewater management (gravity sewers, interceptor systems, reducing I/I). The project explicitly states it 'meets Green Project Reserve criteria' but this appears to be a financing/program designation rather than a separate entry. The project also mentions energy efficiency benefits ('making the system more energy efficient'), but the primary category is Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management. No other ICMA categories are clearly represented in this single project description.
This is a single water/wastewater infrastructure project (sewer system rehabilitation, lift stations, force main) with affected population of 8,750. The project involves wastewater conveyance system improvements to handle increased flow from Steel Dynamics Inc. expansion. Mapped to 'Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management' based on project type (sewer system, wastewater treatment infrastructure). No other ICMA categories are represented on this page.
This is a wastewater infrastructure project (CWSRF - Clean Water State Revolving Fund) with clear 'Affected Population: 1,284' data. The project involves wastewater collection and treatment systems, secondary treatment, and new collector sewers. This clearly maps to 'Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management' based on: (1) keyword match with 'Water and Wastewater', (2) project type (wastewater treatment plant, sewer systems), and (3) NIMS categories indicating water infrastructure. The existing entry 'Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management' with $11,750 amount is the appropriate match. No other ICMA categories (Energy Efficiency, Pollution Prevention and Control, Green Project Reserve) are relevant for this wastewater project. Year 2019 derived from 'CWSRF Annual Report SFY 2019' footer.
This is a wastewater/sanitary sewer project for the White Estates Subdivision in New Castle. The project involves installing grinder pump stations and low pressure sanitary sewer piping to collect wastewater for treatment. The 'Affected Population' of 18,114 represents beneficiaries reached by this water/wastewater infrastructure project. The project amount of $2,543,123 (Total Loan and Principle Forgiveness) should be allocated to Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management. The project closed in November 2018, reported in SFY 2019 Annual Report.
This Evansville A and B project is a combined sewer overflow (CSO) correction project with wastewater treatment plant improvements. The 'Affected Population' of 117,429 represents beneficiaries. The project spans multiple categories: Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management (wastewater treatment), Pollution Prevention and Control (reducing E. coli and dioxins in Ohio River), and Green Project Reserve (explicitly stated as meeting Green Project Reserve criteria and qualifying for 35-year loan term). Energy Efficiency is mentioned as a co-benefit ('more energy efficient system') but is not the primary project category. Project amount of $107,355,000 total loan value.
This is a wastewater system improvement project for Prince's Lakes with affected population of 1,312. The project involves advanced treatment ($870,847) and sewer system rehabilitation ($1,470,153). Based on the project description focusing on wastewater treatment, phosphorus removal, and sewer system improvements, this clearly maps to 'Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management'. The 'Affected Population' of 1,312 represents the beneficiaries reached by this project. Report is CWSRF Annual Report SFY 2019, so year is 2019.
This is a water/wastewater infrastructure project (sanitary sewer extension) that serves 6,728 people. The project eliminates failing septic systems and improves water quality in Vernon Fork. The 'Affected Population' of 6,728 maps to beneficiaries. The project amount is $4,210,000 which is part of the Sustainable Water and Wastewater Management category. Report is from SFY 2019 (State Fiscal Year 2019), so using 2019 as the year.

A Climate Change Vulnerability assessment was completed in March 2024 and informed development of the comprehensive Climate Change Resilience Plan released in September 2024; The Emergency Response Plan is kept up to date; System reliability metrics are tracked to ensure accountability; targets include duration and frequency of service interruptions under varying conditions

Upgrades to physical infrastructure, including projects financed by the 2025TE-1 Restructuring Bonds, enhance grid resilience and include: Utility poles strengthened to withstand high winds; Upsized transformers to increase capacity to handle extreme heat; Automated sectionalizing switches to limit impacts of localized outages; Mitigate flood risk with seawalls or relocate infrastructure; Undergrounding transmission cables to add redundant storm-hardened paths to substations; Vegetation management program to reduce outages caused by trees and vines

Investments in Advanced Distribution Management System software improve outage detection and response times for service restoration; Online monitoring systems for key assets such as substation transformers provide real-time indicators of performance and maintenance needs; Advanced metering infrastructure provides meter-level voltage monitoring and helps avoid low-voltage issues on high-heat days

Extraction notes:
Extracted 3 projects from the table with total allocation of 100,000万元 (1 billion CNY). Project amounts: 湘钢烧结机环保及技术提质改造项目 ~40,000万元, 湘钢4.3米焦炉环保提质改造项目 ~27,800万元, 阳春新钢余热资源综合利用发电项目 ~32,200万元. Two projects mapped to Energy Efficiency (Industrial Energy Efficiency subcategory), one to Renewable Energy (Waste Heat Recovery subcategory). All located in China.
Table shows 2 projects with green industry category classifications per China's Green Bond Endorsed Project Catalogue. Row 3 (阳春新钢铁余热资源综合利用发电项目) maps to 'Energy Efficiency' (waste heat/pressure utilization - 余热余压利用). Row 4 (湖南华菱涟源钢铁4.3米焦炉环保升级改造工程项目) maps to 'Pollution Prevention and Control' (ultra-low emission transformation - 超低排放改造). No monetary amounts or KPI values shown in this table; amounts would need to be sourced from allocation tables elsewhere in report. Project names: Yangchun New Steel Waste Heat Resource Comprehensive Utilization Power Generation Project; Hunan Valin Lianyuan Steel 4.3m Coke Oven Environmental Protection Upgrade and Transformation Project.
Table 8 shows CO2 reductions for three projects under the 2022 first tranche green bond: Xiangtan sintering (1.74万吨=17,400 tCO2e), Xiangtan coke oven (2.18万吨=21,800 tCO2e), and Yangchun waste heat power generation (5.56万吨=55,600 tCO2e). Total 9.49万吨=94,900 tCO2e. The Yangchun project is waste heat power generation (renewable energy), mapped to 'Renewable Energy' entry. The other two are energy efficiency projects. The agent mentioned looking for power generation in 万kWh but this value is not visible in the provided page. Only ghg_avoided could be extracted with certainty. Year 2022 is from the reporting date '截至2022年12月31日'.
Page shows annual CO2 reduction of 9.49万吨 (94,900 tCO2e) for the 2022 first phase green bond, with project breakdown: Xiangtan sintering 1.74万吨 (17,400 tCO2e), Xiangtan coke oven 2.18万吨 (21,800 tCO2e), and Yangchun waste heat 5.56万吨 (55,600 tCO2e). The waste heat project also generates power - 5.56万吨 CO2 corresponds to waste heat power generation. Mapped to Energy Efficiency for the overall GHG avoided and to Renewable Energy for the waste heat power generation component with associated GHG avoided. The table shows 2022 data for the second phase bond with 1.82万吨 CO2 reduction (18,200 tCO2e) for the Lianyuan coke oven project.
